title: Your first class
instruction:
A class defines fields (data) and methods (behaviour).

The `constructor` runs when you do `new ClassName()`. `this` refers to the current object being created.

Run this — notice how each object has its own data.
successMessage: Two Dog objects, each with their own name/breed/age. The class is the blueprint; new Dog(...) creates an instance. This.name = name assigns the constructor parameter to the field.

title: Add a method to your class
instruction:
Add `deposit(double amount)` and `getBalance()` methods to `BankAccount`.

Methods that work on the object's own fields don't need parameters for the fields — they access them directly via `this` (or just the field name).
successMessage: $425.00 — 100 + 250 + 75. Instance methods access fields directly. The object carries its state — balance persists between calls.

title: toString — readable objects
hint: @Override public String toString() { return "Student{name='" + name + "', grade=" + grade + "}"; }
successMessage: @Override tells Java and your IDE that you're replacing a method from the parent class (Object). Without toString(), you'd see the memory address. Every class you create should have one.

title: Multiple constructors
instruction:
Like methods, constructors can be overloaded — multiple constructors with different parameters.

A common pattern: one full constructor and one that provides defaults.
successMessage: this(0, 0) calls the other constructor — constructor chaining. Distance from (0,0) to (3,4) is 5.0 — the 3-4-5 right triangle. Math.sqrt is in java.lang, no import needed.

title: Object references and null
instruction:
Variables hold **references** to objects, not the objects themselves.

`null` means the variable holds no reference — accessing its methods throws `NullPointerException` — Java's most infamous error.

Always check for null before using an object.
successMessage: u1 and u2 point to the same object — modifying via u2 changes u1. This reference semantics is critical: Java objects are always accessed via references. Always null-check before use.

title: Build a Product class
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build a `Product` class:
- Fields: `String name`, `double price`, `int stock`
- Constructor for all three fields
- `sell(int quantity)` — reduces stock, throws `IllegalStateException` if insufficient stock
- `restock(int quantity)` — adds to stock
- `isAvailable()` — returns true if stock > 0
- `toString()` — `"Product{name='...', price=$..., stock=...}"`

Create 2 products, sell some, restock one, print each.
hint: if (quantity > stock) throw new IllegalStateException("Insufficient stock");

codeChecks.message: Throw an exception when stock is insufficient.

successMessage: A complete Product class with validation. Real e-commerce systems use exactly this pattern — sell() checks stock before committing. Chapter 9 done!
